content.text: Give complete proofs and computations. Partial credit will be given where justified.

body.text: Let \(G\) be a region and \(u:G\to\mathbb{R}\) be harmonic. Let \(v\) be a harmonic conjugate to \(u\) in \(G\). Show that the product \(u\cdot v\) is harmonic.

subparts.label: (a)
subparts.text:
Show that if \(f\) is analytic on the closed disk \(\overline{B(0;1)}\), then
\[
|f(a)|^2\leq \frac{1}{\pi R^2}\int_0^{2\pi}\int_0^R|f(a+re^{i\theta})|^2r\,dr\,d\theta.
\]

subparts.label: (b)
subparts.text:
Let \(G\) be a region and \(M\in\mathbb{R}\) be fixed. Let
\[
\mathcal{F}=\left\{f\in H(G)\mathrel{\bigg|}\int_G|f(z)|^2\,dx\,dy\leq M\right\}.
\]
Show that \(\mathcal{F}\) is a normal family.

body.text:
Let \(f(z)=\sum_{n=0}^{\infty}a_nz^n\) and \(g(z)=\sum_{n=0}^{\infty}b_nz^n\) be analytic on \(\overline{B(0;1)}\). Let
\[
F(z)=\frac{1}{2\pi i}\int_{\partial B(0;1)}\frac{f(w)}{w}g\left(\frac{z}{w}\right)\,dw,\qquad z\in B(0;1),
\]
with the contour taken with counterclockwise orientation. Show that
\[
F(z)=\sum_{n=0}^{\infty}a_nb_nz^n,\qquad z\in B(0;1).
\]

subparts.label: (a)
subparts.text: Must there exist a sequence of polynomials \(P_n\) such that \(P_n\to f\) uniformly on compact subsets of \(G\)? Explain your reasoning.

subparts.label: (b)
subparts.text: Must there exist such a sequence which converges to \(f\) uniformly on \(G\)? Explain your reasoning.

subparts.label: (c)
subparts.text: Must there exist such a sequence which converges to \(f\) uniformly on \(G\), if \(f\) is now required to be analytic in some neighborhood of \(\overline{G}\)? Explain your reasoning.

body.text: Let \(G\) be a region and \(A\) be a discrete and relatively closed subset of \(G\). Assume that \(f\in H(G\setminus A)\) is injective.

subparts.label: (a)
subparts.text: Prove that no point of \(A\) can be an essential singularity of \(f\).

subparts.label: (b)
subparts.text: Prove that if \(a\in A\) is a pole for \(f\), then \(a\) is a pole of order 1.

body.text: Let \(f\) be analytic on \(\overline{B(0;1)}\). If \(|f(z)|<1\) on \(\partial B(0;1)\), prove that there exists a unique \(z_0\in B(0;1)\) (counting multiplicity) such that \(f(z_0)=z_0\). If \(|f(z)|\leq 1\) on \(\partial B(0;1)\), does the same conclusion hold? Justify your assertion.
